text: Pressing and holding the button allows the rotation of the entire robotic boom.
The video shows two buttons attached to each of the robotic arms, highlighting their placement and potential function in controlling the arm movements.
A button located at the bottom of the robotic arm's 'hand' allows for side-to-side and up-and-down movement.
The video shows a button located on the upper part of the robotic arm, which allows the arm to angle left, right, forward, and backward.
The movement of the robotic arm is controlled directly from the elbow joint, allowing precise adjustments in positioning.
The robotic arms are positioned close to the patient during the procedure. The patient clearance button is used to adjust the arm positions, ensuring they do not come into contact with the patient.

The camera focuses on the robotic arms, highlighting several small red dots on the top of the arms, which are magnets used for draping purposes. A large clear plastic drape is shown being used with these magnets.
The drape is placed over the robotic arm, extending along its length. Two magnets are held underneath the drape and snapped into place on the magnet holes located on the arm.
The drape, already formed into a square shape, is positioned onto the tower by holding underneath and aligning it with the magnet.
The individual holds the outside of the drape, tears the tapes, and pulls the drape down, ensuring to handle only the exterior of the drape.
The tower drape is visible, followed by the preparation for placing the arm drape.
The drape is positioned over the robotic arm, with visible magnets above two hand-shaped markings on the drape. The surgeon slides both hands into the designated cups on the drape.
The drape is pulled up along the robotic arm, and the magnet pad is attached securely at the top.

The surgeon pulls the slack out of the drape and undoes the tape, bringing it down slightly. The top and bottom plastic pieces are attached, ensuring no excess drape is trapped underneath to prevent docking faults.
The surgeon presses malleable components of the drape backwards to keep it smooth and unobstructed, facilitating easy insertion of instruments into the trocars.

The surgeon adjusts the malleable plastic pieces on the robotic arm to prevent the drape from obstructing instrument insertion. The final step involves sliding and clamping the plastic piece on the back side of the drape to minimize slack and ensure it remains flat, avoiding contamination risks.
